raw_case:
Imaging in Zinner Syndrome, A Case Series: The Wolf in Sheep’s Clothing
Zinner syndrome is a rare congenital malformation of the Wolffian duct with seminal vesicle cyst, ipsilateral renal agenesis and ejaculatory duct obstruction, seen in less than 0.002 % of the male population. An increased awareness among clinicians, urologists, and radiologists is essential, as early diagnosis and management can impact fertility outcomes. This review aims to explain and illustrate Zinner syndrome through imaging case studies. Emphasis is made on considering Zinner syndrome in the differential diagnosis of young males with pelvic pain and renal agenesis, highlighting the importance of radiological evaluation for the management of this uncommon yet clinically significant condition.

Highlights

•The classic triad of Zinner's syndrome consists of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cysts, and ejaculatory duct obstruction.•Transabdominal ultrasound, TRUS, and MRI are essential modalities to reach an accurate diagnosis.•Symptomatic patients benefit from surgical intervention, with recent advancements in laparoscopic and robotic-assisted techniques providing optimism for better outcomes.•Detailed and timely imaging, clinical expertise and early intervention is crucial to diagnose and manage such unique conditions.

Abbreviations:

CTComputed tomographyH&EHematoxylin and EosinLIFLeft Iliac FossaMRIMagnetic Resonance ImagingOPDOut-Patient DepartmentTASTransabdominal Ultrasound ScanSTIRShort Tau Inversion RecoveryTRUSTransrectal Ultrasound ScanUSG –UltrasoundUTIUrinary Tract InfectionVURVesicoureteric Reflux1

Introduction

Zinner syndrome was first described by Zinner in 1914. It is an unusual congenital Wolffian duct malformation characterized by the triad of seminal vesicle cysts, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction. This condition's incidence is estimated to be less than 0.002 % in the male population with only a handful of 200 cases reported to date.1 Normally, both the ureteral buds and seminal vesicles arise from the mesonephric duct. A defect in the formation of the distal/dorsal mesonephric duct results in atresia of the ejaculatory duct, causing obstruction and dilatation of the seminal vesicle.2 Increasing awareness among clinicians, urologists, surgeons, and radiologists is crucial due to the significant clinical and fertility implications of Zinner syndrome. Timely and accurate diagnosis through multimodal imaging techniques is essential to guide appropriate therapeutic interventions and improve patient outcomes, reproductive health, and quality of life.32

Case presentation

The first patient is a 38 year old male who presented to the Out-Patient Department (OPD) with symptoms of intermittent perineal and pelvic pain, on and off fever since 8 months, dysuria with burning micturition, occasional haematuria, hematospermia confirmed by urine and semen analysis which also showed oligospermia (3,500,000/milliliter) with reduced volume. Marital history revealed secondary infertility and recent onset painful ejaculation. In view of above clinical findings, a TRUS was advised in suspicion of prostatitis.TRUS revealed a large complex multilocular cystic lesion with internal echogenic contents, septations and fluid levels arising from the right seminal vesicle. The left seminal vesicle appeared bulky and edematous suggestive of vesiculitis. Dilated right more than left vas deferens secondary to dilated & obstructed distal ejaculatory duct were observed. The proximal ejaculatory duct could not be visualized. Prostate appeared edematous, showing heterogenous echotexture, periprostatic oedema and increased vascularity due to prostatitis (Fig. 1).A provisional diagnosis on TRUS was suggestive of right complex seminal vesicle cyst. A differential diagnosis of right seminal vesicle abscess was also considered. Patient underwent an extensive antibiotic course and came for a follow up scan after 1 month with no significant interval change in the volume of the cystic lesion. However, there was some intermittent improvement in his clinical symptoms with no further worsening. Patient was further planned for TRUS guided aspiration and drainage.However, an out of the box follow-up scan with a vigilant and timely abdominal ultrasound beyond the eyes and need of the clinical symptoms was done which incidentally revealed an absent right kidney with compensatory hypertrophy of the left kidney (Fig. 2). Now, a diagnosis of an associated Wolffian duct abnormality was made and MRI pelvis was suggested to confirm the diagnosis.MRI of the abdomen and pelvis showed a large complex multilocular cystic lesion appearing T2 hyperintense with no diffusion restriction. T1 hyperintense fluid levels were noted within suggestive of haemorrhagic contents. The cyst was arising from the right seminal vesicle and abutting the bladder, right iliac vessels and levator ani. Left seminal vesicle appeared mildly bulky, edematous with T2 hyperintensity and few small cystic changes suggestive of vesiculitis. Absent right kidney with compensatory hypertrophy of left kidney was suggestive of renal agenesis. Thorax screening showed no obvious ectopic kidney. Also, the prostate appeared heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted sequence with periprostatic oedema and few tiny cystic areas in the periphery with no diffusion restriction suggestive of prostatitis (Fig. 3). A final diagnosis of Zinners syndrome was made.The second patient is a 26 year old male who presented with recurrent UTI and diffuse lower abdominal pain. TAS and TRUS showed a tubular dilated fluid filled structure arising from the region of left seminal vesicle extending from the pelvis to LIF. Small anechoic fluid filled outpouchings were seen at the anterior wall of urinary bladder. The prostate volume was low for age and bilateral vas deferens were dilated secondary to ejaculatory duct obstruction. There was associated left renal agenesis (Fig. 4).MRI confirmed the diagnosis of a wolffian duct abnormality variant (Zinner's syndrome). A tubulocystic lesion arising from left seminal vesicle with blind ending in LIF was possibly suggestive of an ectopic megaureter remnant. Absent left kidney was suggestive of agenesis. Small fluid signal outpouchings at anterior wall of bladder hinted at rudimentary ectopic ureters. Prostatic hypoplasia & dilated vas deferens were also seen (Fig. 5).The imaging findings for both cases were confirmed post laproscopic excision of the cyst indicating high accuracy. Both the patients showed significant resolution of the symptoms post surgery. There was no obvious recurrence of the cysts on follow up USG in both.There was an improved sperm count (5,400,000/milliliter) in the first patient at 8 months follow up. Histopathological examination of the tubulocyst from second case revealed transitional epithelial lining, muscularis and adventitia layers suggestive of an ureter remnant (Fig. 5-F).3

Discussion

Patients usually present during the third to fifth decade of life with complaints of lower abdominal pain, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), perineal pain, recurrent prostatitis, hematospermia, painful ejaculation, and infertility.4 Atypical symptoms, including intermittent scrotal pain and prostatic utricle cysts, have also been observed.Many other developmental abnormalities are associated with this pathology, such as congenital absence of the vas deferens, ipsilateral ectopic ureter, rudimentary ureter, megaureter, contralateral renal anomalies, cryptorchidism, vesicoureteric reflux, testicular hypoplasia, Müllerian duct abnormalities and prostatic abnormalities including cysts.5 It is also scarcely reported to be associated with renal cell carcinoma, prostatic adenocarcinoma, and testicular germ cell tumours.6 A rare and unique instance of villous adenoma of the colon associated with congenital seminal vesicle cyst has also been reported.7Transabdominal ultrasound is the initial imaging modality that reveals renal agenesis and pelvic cysts. Transrectal ultrasound is extremely valuable in evaluating the seminal vesicles and prostate. The complexity of the cyst (multi-locules, septations, nodules, vascularity, calcification, wall thickness, internal contents), associated prostatitis, or ejaculatory duct obstruction can be better assessed. It is also useful in guided biopsies or aspirations if needed. CT urography helps in the detailed evaluation of the urinary tract and associated anomalies, such as vesicoureteral reflux. It helps in the detection of secondary complications like hydronephrosis or infections. A contrast study can help differentiate cystic and solid masses, aiding in the exclusion of neoplasm and in the visualization of vascular structures in relation to the cyst.8MRI pelvis is the gold standard investigation, helping in the precise characterization of the nature of seminal vesicle cysts. T2-weighted images highlight the fluid-filled cysts while T1-weighted images can identify haemorrhagic contents within. Other associated anomalies involving the ureters, prostate, testes, kidneys and Müllerian duct derivatives can be evaluated.9 Nuclear medicine scans can help assess renal function and confirm the absence of the kidney. Early and accurate imaging diagnosis is essential for appropriate management.Treatment depends on the symptomatology. Asymptomatic patients are managed conservatively while symptomatic patients are managed surgically with transperitoneal laparoscopic cyst excision. Laparoscopic unroofing was better than fenestration under vesiculoscopy in terms of symptoms and fertility outcome.10 The surgical criteria for cyst excision is generally less than 10 cm. However, a 12.5 cm seminal vesicle cyst was excised without any complication in one study.11 More recently, robotic assistance has been used in the excision and draining of these cysts with wall marsupialization to prevent recurrence. The prognosis of Zinner syndrome is generally favourable, particularly when the condition is diagnosed early and managed appropriately. There is a low risk of recurrence when the cyst is fully excised.124

Conclusion

A skillful, detailed and prompt radiological imaging coupled with clinical expertise is essential to diagnose such unique and rare conditions as Zinner syndrome. Early intervention may improve fertility outcomes and quality of life for patients.

Availability of data and material

Not applicable.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Sanjay: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. S. Tanuj Sai Kumar: Investigation, Data curation.

Patient consent

Written informed consent has been obtained for publication. The images are anonymized to protect patient identity and privacy.

Financial Support

Nil.

Use of artificial intelligence

No.

Funding

Nil.

Conflicts of interest

No.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 38-year-old male (First case), 26-year-old male (Second case)
- Chief Complaint: 
  - First case: Intermittent perineal and pelvic pain, dysuria, burning micturition, occasional hematuria, hematospermia, painful ejaculation, secondary infertility.
  - Second case: Recurrent urinary tract infections (UTI) and lower abdominal pain.
- History of Present Illness: 
  - First case: Symptoms present for 8 months, semen analysis revealed oligospermia and reduced ejaculate volume.
  - Second case: Recurrent UTIs and vague abdominal symptoms.
- Past Medical History: None mentioned.
- Personal History: None mentioned.
- Family History: None mentioned.
- Physical Examination: None provided.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - First case:
    - Transrectal Ultrasound (TRUS): Multiloculated cystic lesion in right seminal vesicle, edematous prostate showing features of prostatitis, dilated vas deferens and ejaculatory duct obstruction.
    - Abdominal Ultrasound: Absent right kidney with compensatory hypertrophy of left kidney.
    - MRI: Large complex cyst in seminal vesicle, renal agenesis, features of prostatitis, and vesiculitis in left seminal vesicle.
    - Post-surgery improvement noted with sperm count increase during follow-up (5,400,000/milliliter).
  - Second case:
    - Transabdominal Ultrasound (TAS) and TRUS: Cystic left seminal vesicle lesion, ectopic ureter remnants, left renal agenesis, prostatic hypoplasia, and ejaculatory duct obstruction.
    - MRI: Tubulocystic lesion in left seminal vesicle, absence of left kidney, rudimentary ectopic ureters, prostatic hypoplasia, and dilated vas deferens.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- Initial considerations for patient symptoms included conditions like seminal vesicle abscess, prostatitis, or renal malformations.
- For the first patient:
  - Seminal vesicle abscess was considered but excluded due to the persistent cystic lesion despite antibiotic therapy and absence of significant response.
  - Zinner syndrome was suspected based on identifying a triad of seminal vesicle cyst, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction through imaging tests. MRI findings confirmed the diagnosis with detailed characterization of seminal vesicle cystic nature and pelvic anatomy.
- For the second patient:
  - Similar to the first patient, imaging findings of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cystic abnormality, and ejaculatory duct obstruction pointed to Zinner syndrome.
  - Tubulocystic lesions and ectopic remnants were consistent with Wolffian duct abnormalities.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Zinner syndrome (both cases), characterized by seminal vesicle cyst, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction. The diagnosis was confirmed through multimodal imaging techniques, and surgical intervention resulted in symptomatic improvement.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. So, we have two male patients presenting with urogenital issues. Both have some similar findings. Let me start with the first case.

The first patient is a 38-year-old male with perineal and pelvic pain, dysuria, burning on urination, hematuria, hematospermia, painful ejaculation, and secondary infertility. His history includes 8 months of symptoms, and semen analysis shows oligospermia and reduced volume. The tests here are interesting. The TRUS and MRI show a cystic lesion in the right seminal vesicle, prostatitis, dilated vas deferens, ejaculatory duct obstruction, and importantly, an absent right kidney with left kidney hypertrophy. Post-surgery, his sperm count improved. 

Now, the second case is a 26-year-old with recurrent UTIs and lower abdominal pain. His imaging shows a cystic lesion in the left seminal vesicle, left renal agenesis, ectopic ureter remnants, prostatic hypoplasia, and ejaculatory duct obstruction. 

Hmm, both patients have renal agenesis on the same side as the seminal vesicle cysts. The first case's right kidney is absent with a right seminal vesicle cyst, the second has left renal agenesis and left seminal vesicle issues. There's also ectopic ureter remnants mentioned in the second case. 

Wait, renal agenesis is a congenital anomaly. The association between renal agenesis and seminal vesicle cysts... I remember there's a condition called Zinner syndrome. Let me recall: Zinner syndrome is a rare congenital disorder characterized by the triad of ipsilateral renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cyst, and ejaculatory duct obstruction. It's a Müllerian duct anomaly. Since the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct gives rise to the kidneys, ureters, and seminal vesicles, a developmental issue here could lead to renal agenesis and abnormalities in the seminal vesicles. 

In both cases, the patients have renal agenesis and ipsilateral seminal vesicle cysts. The first case also has prostatitis and vesiculitis, which might be secondary to obstruction leading to infection. The second case has ectopic ureter remnants, which can be part of the same developmental anomaly. The ejaculatory duct obstruction would explain the oligospermia and infertility in the first case, as well as the recurrent UTIs in the second due to possible stasis or reflux.

Other possibilities could include isolated seminal vesicle cysts, but the presence of renal agenesis points towards a congenital syndrome. Prostatitis and vesiculitis are likely secondary to the obstruction. The improvement post-surgery in the first case, where they probably addressed the cyst or obstruction, supports that the primary issue is structural. 

So putting it all together, both cases fit Zinner syndrome. Each has the triad of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cyst, and ejaculatory duct obstruction. The first case had surgery which helped, which aligns with treatment for Zinner's where draining the cyst or relieving obstruction can improve symptoms and fertility.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> Both patients present with congenital renal agenesis (right in the first case, left in the second) and ipsilateral seminal vesicle cystic lesions. Renal agenesis is a key congenital anomaly linked to developmental defects in the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct system.
  
<step 2> The seminal vesicle cysts and associated findings (ectopic ureter remnants, ejaculatory duct obstruction, vas deferens dilation) align with abnormal development of structures derived from the mesonephric duct, which also gives rise to the kidneys. This suggests a congenital malformation syndrome.

<step 3> The combination of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cyst, and ejaculatory duct obstruction is pathognomonic for Zinner syndrome, a rare congenital disorder caused by maldevelopment of the mesonephric duct. Secondary prostatitis, vesiculitis, and infertility are common complications due to obstruction and stasis.

<step 4> Both cases lack a functional kidney on the affected side, show compensatory contralateral hypertrophy, and exhibit structural abnormalities in the seminal vesicles and adjacent ducts, excluding isolated prostatitis or acquired seminal vesicle cysts. The recurrent UTIs in the second case likely stem from urinary stasis or reflux due to ductal anomalies.

<step 5> Post-surgical improvement in sperm count (first case) further supports obstructive pathology as the primary etiology, consistent with Zinner syndrome management outcomes after cyst drainage or ductal obstruction relief.

### Answer:
Zinner syndrome

reasoning_eval.step: Both patients present with congenital renal agenesis (right in the first case, left in the second) and ipsilateral seminal vesicle cystic lesions. Renal agenesis is a key congenital anomaly linked to developmental defects in the mesonephric (Wolffian) duct system.

The seminal vesicle cysts and associated findings (ectopic ureter remnants, ejaculatory duct obstruction, vas deferens dilation) align with abnormal development of structures derived from the mesonephric duct, which also gives rise to the kidneys. This suggests a congenital malformation syndrome.

The combination of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cyst, and ejaculatory duct obstruction is pathognomonic for Zinner syndrome, a rare congenital disorder caused by maldevelopment of the mesonephric duct. Secondary prostatitis, vesiculitis, and infertility are common complications due to obstruction and stasis.

Both cases lack a functional kidney on the affected side, show compensatory contralateral hypertrophy, and exhibit structural abnormalities in the seminal vesicles and adjacent ducts, excluding isolated prostatitis or acquired seminal vesicle cysts. The recurrent UTIs in the second case likely stem from urinary stasis or reflux due to ductal anomalies.

Post-surgical improvement in sperm count (first case) further supports obstructive pathology as the primary etiology, consistent with Zinner syndrome management outcomes after cyst drainage or ductal obstruction relief.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial considerations for patient symptoms included conditions like seminal vesicle abscess, prostatitis, or renal malformations.
For the first patient, seminal vesicle abscess was considered but excluded due to the persistent cystic lesion despite antibiotic therapy and absence of significant response.
Zinner syndrome was suspected based on identifying a triad of seminal vesicle cyst, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction through imaging tests.
MRI findings confirmed the diagnosis with detailed characterization of seminal vesicle cystic nature and pelvic anatomy.
For the second patient, similar imaging findings of renal agenesis, seminal vesicle cystic abnormality, and ejaculatory duct obstruction pointed to Zinner syndrome.
Tubulocystic lesions and ectopic remnants were consistent with Wolffian duct abnormalities.
Final diagnosis: Zinner syndrome (both cases), characterized by seminal vesicle cyst, ipsilateral renal agenesis, and ejaculatory duct obstruction.
The diagnosis was confirmed through multimodal imaging techniques.
Surgical intervention resulted in symptomatic improvement.
